Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-12-04T14:09:26Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T20:17:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/25307","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25307","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25307.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/25307.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"The `crackfortran` tool is developed to be a backend for the f2py utility. However, crackfortran could also be very useful on its own.

The use of crackfortran could be demonstrated by the following example:

```
► crackfortran symbol.f90 -show
Reading fortran codes...
        Reading file 'symbol.f90' (format:free)
Post-processing...
        Block: symbol
Post-processing (stage 2)...
[{'args': ['x', 'y', 'symx', 'opt'],
  'block': 'subroutine',
  'body': [],
  'common': {'pltdat': ['model', 'ploter'], 'symbls': ['nsym', 'symbl']},
  'commonvars': ['model', 'ploter', 'nsym', 'symbl'],
  'entry': {},
  'externals': [],
  'from': 'symbol.f90',
  'implicit': None,
  'interfaced': [],
  'name': 'symbol',
  'sortvars': ['model', 'nsym', 'ploter', 'symbl', 'opt', 'x', 'y', 'symx'],
  'vars': {'model': {'attrspec': [], 'typespec': 'integer'},
           'nsym': {'attrspec': [], 'typespec': 'integer'},
           'opt': {'typespec': 'integer'},
           'ploter': {'attrspec': [], 'typespec': 'integer'},
           'symbl': {'attrspec': [],
                     'dimension': ['20', '2'],
                     'typespec': 'integer'},
           'symx': {'attrspec': [], 'dimension': ['2'], 'typespec': 'integer'},
           'x': {'typespec': 'real'},
           'y': {'typespec': 'real'}}}]
```

The tool presents the subroutine declaration statements as a JSON that can be consumed by other tools for further analysis.

Everyone who is looking for the Fortran parser should first come across crackfortran, before wasting time on many sloppy half-working parsers on the Internet. In this regard, the importance of crackfortran is currently misrepresented by keeping it in the depth of numpy internals. Therefore, this patch adds changes required to expose crackfortran as an entry point, next to f2py3 and reveal it to the world.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/python-blosc/issues/322","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/python-blosc","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/python-blosc/issues/322/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/python-blosc/issues/322/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/python-blosc/issues/322/events","html_url":"https://github.com/Blosc/python-blosc/issues/322","id":2104367349,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA6Dms59bhj1","number":322,"title":"__pack_tensor__ must be made portable and not depend on Python","user":{"login":"dmikushin","id":5672874,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NzI4NzQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5672874?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin","html_url":"https://github.com/dmikushin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/dmikushin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-28T22:01:22Z","updated_at":"2024-01-28T22:01:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi @FrancescAlted , @lbolla and everyone!

Blosc2 is the data communication language of the future! Overall, Blosc2 is very portable, but I've recently stumbled upon one questionable aspect.

Blosc2 communicated tensor shapes using "__pack_tensor__" variable-length metadata token. To my surprise, this token content is a **Python object** serialized with msgpack. This means that even if I want to use Blosc2 data frames in a purely C++ application, I need to bring up the whole Python interpreter to read the content of "__pack_tensor__".

In fact, "__pack_tensor__" does not require Python. It's a string for kind, then an array of integers for shape, and again a string for data type. I believe all of these can be expressed in plain data without involving Python, which would allow Blosc2 to be truly portable.

It's just a historical matter that python-blosc and PyTables are the first two practical applications of Blosc. Therefore, they standardize in their own way some aspects that should be the part of the abstract core design. I mean, "__pack_tensor__" should be a part of c-blosc2, and my question should rather apply there; c-blosc2 should define a universal form of "__pack_tensor__", which all higher-level applications should conform to.

Blosc2 is the data communication language of the future! Overall, Blosc2 is very portable, but I've recently stumbled upon one questionable aspect.

Blosc2 communicates tensor shapes using `__pack_tensor__` variable-length metadata token. To my surprise, this token content is a **Python object** serialized with msgpack. This means that even if I want to use Blosc2 data frames in a purely C++ application, I need to bring up the whole Python interpreter to read the content of `__pack_tensor__`.

In fact, `__pack_tensor__` should not require Python. It's a string for kind, then an array of integers for shape, and again a string for data type. I believe all of these can be expressed in plain data without involving Python, which would allow Blosc2 to be truly portable.

It's just a historical matter that python-blosc and PyTables are the first two practical applications of Blosc. Therefore, they standardize in their own way some aspects that should rather be a part of the abstract core design. I mean, `__pack_tensor__` should be a part of c-blosc2, and my question should rather apply there; c-blosc2 should define a universal form of `__pack_tensor__`, which all higher-level applications should conform to.

The use of crackfortran could be demonstrated by the following example:

```
► crackfortran symbol.f90 -show
Reading fortran codes...

           'opt': {'typespec': 'integer'},
           'ploter': {'attrspec': [], 'typespec': 'integer'},
           'symbl': {'attrspec': [],
                     'dimension': ['20', '2'],
                     'typespec': 'integer'},
           'symx': {'attrspec': [], 'dimension': ['2'], 'typespec': 'integer'},
           'x': {'typespec': 'real'},
           'y': {'typespec': 'real'}}}]
```

The tool presents the subroutine declaration statements as a JSON that can be consumed by other tools for further analysis.

Everyone who is looking for the Fortran parser should first come accross crackfortran, before wasting time on many sloppy half-working parsers on the Internet. In this regard, the importance of crackfortran is currently misrepresented by keeping it in the depth of numpy internals. Therefore, this patch adds changes required to expose crackfortran as an entry point, next to f2py3 and reveal it to the world.

CuPy doesn't seem to utilize the memory efficient data structure of sparse matrices. It stores the whole array.
![Screenshot from 2023-04-22 19-42-20](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86722560/233800756-eeebafa5-16ff-474f-84e3-f4360b067162.png)
This is what it should look like and does with SciPy.
![Screenshot from 2023-04-22 19-47-06](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86722560/233800791-9670dd4b-bb07-430f-9f3c-0f99e13db3c4.png)
It uses slightly more memory and is slower then using a normal CuPy array even though the matrix is very sparse.


### To Reproduce

```py
# Creat a sparse matrix e.g.
from cupyx.scipy.sparse import random
data = random(1000, 1000)
```


### Installation

Wheel (`pip install cupy-***`)

```
import scipy.sparse
import cupy as cp
import cupyx as cpx

m = n = 1000

#create A random sparse matrix

A_gpu = cpx.scipy.sparse.csr_matrix(A_cpu)
```
